answer:
**Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq's ghazal from *Diwan-E-Zauq*** begins with the matla (opening couplet): "Ab toh ghabra ke yeh kehte hain ke mar jaayenge" ("So frayed with dread, I say that I will die and be gone"). This ghazal, numbered as chapter 4 in the collection, explores themes of overwhelming anxiety, unhealing wounds of love, the futility of existence even in death, and redemption through wine and love over rigid scholarship—evoking a longing for escape or death as relief.[^1]

Here is the full English translation:

> So frayed with dread, I say that I will die and be gone;  
> If even death brings no relief, where then will I be gone?[^2]  
> 
> If you decide the rival's house is where you will be known,  
> Then my own firm resolves will change, and I will be gone.[^3]  
> 
> O Healers, though your arts are spent and all your skills are shown,  
> My wounds are not the kind that heal; they will not be gone.[^4]  
> 
> How can we hope to reach her path, to make her lane our own?  
> Not till we pass beyond both worlds, and finally are gone.[^5]  
> 
> My sigh could flash like lightning from a fiery, angry throne,  
> But I fear she would see the light and, startled, would be gone.[^6]  
> 
> I'm not the one to blame you for my final, dying groan;  
> In fact, if God himself should ask, I'll lie and be gone.[^7]  
> 
> The very fires of Hell itself will lose their burning tone,  
> When this poor sinner, drenched in shame, has finally been gone.[^8]  
> 
> No one will ever find a trace of me, no shard or bone,  
> When swift as a glance's flight from here, I will be gone.[^9]  
> 
> Tell all the rivers, when they see my pearl-shedding eyes alone,  
> If they should rise in flood, they'll fall from grace and will be gone.[^10]  
> 
> Should drunken friends bring their red eyes to my cold grave-stone,  
> At least two flowers will be laid there, and then they will be gone.[^11]  
> 
> Just lift the veil from your bright face, make its true radiance known;  
> The sun and moon will lose all worth, their glory will be gone.[^12]  
> 
> I too shall see if any have true vision of their own,  
> When swift as an arrow-glance from here, I finally am gone.[^13]  
> 
> Zauq, those scholars whom the rigid schools have left to groan,  
> Bring them to the tavern's heart; they'll mend and be gone.[^14]

For the Roman transliteration (standardized Latin script) and original Urdu (Arabic script), see the chapter pages linked below.[^15][^16]

[^1]: *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 4 ("Ab toh ghabra ke yeh kehte hain ke mar jaayenge"), [/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., [p-c45524](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/#p-c45524).

[^3]: Ibid., [p-ce8437](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/#p-ce8437).

[^4]: Ibid., [p-6b6af7](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/#p-6b6af7).

args.query: "Ab to ghabra ke ye kehte hain"
